# Markdown Hierarchy Parser


<!-- WARNING: THIS FILE WAS AUTOGENERATED! DO NOT EDIT! -->

``` python
from fastcore.test import *
```

Markdown documents already divide themselves into useful semantic units.
[`HeadingDict`](https://AnswerDotAI.github.io/toolslm/md_hier.html#headingdict)
preserves that hierarchy, so a long document can be inspected by section
instead of searched and sliced as one flat string. Every node is a
dictionary of child headings with the complete Markdown for that section
in `.text`.

## Heading trees

[`HeadingDict`](https://AnswerDotAI.github.io/toolslm/md_hier.html#headingdict)
supports normal dictionary lookup. `at()` follows a heading path in one
call, `paths()` lists every available path, and `find()` returns a
heading when its title occurs exactly once. Explicit paths are
preferable when a title such as “Hooks” appears in more than one place.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<a
href="https://github.com/AnswerDotAI/toolslm/blob/main/toolslm/md_hier.py#L16"
target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

### HeadingDict

``` python
def HeadingDict(
    text:str='', start_line:int=1, *args, **kwargs
):
```

*A dictionary of child headings with the section Markdown in `text`.*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<a
href="https://github.com/AnswerDotAI/toolslm/blob/main/toolslm/md_hier.py#L57"
target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

### create_heading_dict

``` python
def create_heading_dict(
    text, rm_fenced:bool=True
):
```

*Create a nested
[`HeadingDict`](https://AnswerDotAI.github.io/toolslm/md_hier.html#headingdict)
from Markdown headings.*

```` python
sample_md = r'''# Hooks

Shared hook behavior.

## Common input fields

Every hook receives `session_id`.

## Hooks

### SessionStart

Runs at thread start.

```python
# This is code, not a heading
```

### PostCompact

Runs after compaction.'''

result = create_heading_dict(sample_md)
result.paths()
````

    [('Hooks',),
     ('Hooks', 'Common input fields'),
     ('Hooks', 'Hooks'),
     ('Hooks', 'Hooks', 'SessionStart'),
     ('Hooks', 'Hooks', 'PostCompact')]

Use `at()` when you know the path. The returned node includes its
heading and all Markdown beneath it, up to the next heading at the same
or a higher level.

``` python
session = result.at('Hooks', 'Hooks', 'SessionStart')
session.text
```

    '### SessionStart\n\nRuns at thread start.\n\n```python\n# This is code, not a heading\n```'

For non-left nodes, it shows the whole section including lower level:

``` python
hooks = result.at('Hooks', 'Hooks')
hooks.text
```

    '## Hooks\n\n### SessionStart\n\nRuns at thread start.\n\n```python\n# This is code, not a heading\n```\n\n### PostCompact\n\nRuns after compaction.'

`find()` is shorter when a title is unique anywhere in the document. It
raises when the title is absent or ambiguous, so it cannot quietly
select the wrong section.

``` python
test_eq(result.find('SessionStart'), session)
test_fail(lambda: result.find('Hooks'), contains='found 2')
```

## Fenced code and duplicate headings

Markdown examples often contain lines that look like headings. Backtick
and tilde fenced blocks are ignored by default, so `# This is code` does
not appear in the heading tree.

``` python
expected = r'''### SessionStart

Runs at thread start.

```python
# This is code, not a heading
```'''
test_eq(result.find('SessionStart').text, expected)
test_eq([path for path in result.paths() if path[-1] == 'This is code'], [])
```

### Duplicate siblings

A dictionary cannot represent two children with the same key. Rather
than silently discard the first section,
[`create_heading_dict`](https://AnswerDotAI.github.io/toolslm/md_hier.html#create_heading_dict)
raises for duplicate sibling headings. Repeated titles under different
parents are fine and remain addressable by path.

``` python
test_fail(lambda: create_heading_dict('# A\n## Same\n## Same'), contains='Duplicate sibling heading')
test_eq(result.paths()[-1], ('Hooks', 'Hooks', 'PostCompact'))
```

## Line-addressed views

Each node records `start_line`, the 1-based line number of its heading
in the original document, and `view()` can prefix each line of the
section with its absolute line number or with a `lineno|hash|` address.
The address comes from
[`fastcore.tools.lnhash`](https://fastcore.fast.ai/tools.html#lnhash)
(the same CRC-32 formula [exhash](https://github.com/AnswerDotAI/exhash)
uses), so when the parsed text came from a local file, the output of
`view(lnhashs=True)` provides valid exhash addresses for editing that
file directly.

``` python
result.find('PostCompact').view(lnhashs=True)
```

`nums=True` shows plain line numbers instead; `lnhashs` wins when both
are set, and with neither, `view()` is just `text`. A section starts at
its own heading line, so numbering lines up with the full document.

``` python
sec = result.find('PostCompact')
test_eq(sample_md.splitlines()[sec.start_line-1], '### PostCompact')
test_eq(sec.view(lnhashs=True).splitlines()[0], lnhash(sec.start_line, '### PostCompact')+'### PostCompact')
test_eq(sec.view(nums=True).splitlines()[0], f"{sec.start_line}: ### PostCompact")
test_eq(sec.view(nums=True, lnhashs=True), sec.view(lnhashs=True))
test_eq(sec.view(), sec.text)
```

## Files

target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

### create_heading_dict_file

``` python
def create_heading_dict_file(
    path, rm_fenced:bool=True
):
```

*Create a
[`HeadingDict`](https://AnswerDotAI.github.io/toolslm/md_hier.html#headingdict)
from the Markdown file at `path` (`~` ok), recording `path` for
`refresh`*

[`create_heading_dict_file`](https://AnswerDotAI.github.io/toolslm/md_hier.html#create_heading_dict_file)
reads a Markdown file (expanding a leading `~`) and records the path on
the returned root, so the whole local-file workflow needs no raw file
reads. Since a parsed tree persists while the file may change, addresses
can go stale after an edit: `refresh()` re-reads the file and returns a
fresh tree. A stale address fails loudly at exhash’s hash check rather
than editing the wrong line.

``` python
p = Path('tmp_sample.md')
p.write_text(sample_md)
d = create_heading_dict_file('tmp_sample.md')
test_eq(d.find('PostCompact').view(lnhashs=True), result.find('PostCompact').view(lnhashs=True))
test_eq(d.refresh().paths(), d.paths())
p.unlink()
```
